Dear Land F/X Hiring Team,

I am a software engineer with over five years of professional experience building, maintaining, and optimizing web platforms, and I want to bring that experience to Land F/X. Earlier in my career at ICC Innovative Consulting, I helped build An Sinh, a mission-critical social security application supporting nine million citizens during COVID-19. That project demanded clear architecture decisions, careful documentation, and the ability to advocate for technical choices under pressure. I bring that same discipline to every codebase I work in, including writing clean, maintainable code and keeping project notes accurate and current.

Land F/X has built a reputation for outstanding support and a close relationship with its users. I admire that. I want to work somewhere that takes quality seriously at every level, from the code to the customer. I am ready to contribute to your web presence, your client portal, and the systems that keep your users productive.

Sincerely,

I am a software engineer with over five years of professional web development experience, and I want to bring that experience to Land F/X. Your software serves thousands of landscape architects and irrigation designers worldwide, and the care you put into both product quality and client support is evident. I build the same way: with reliability, performance, and the end user in mind.

My current role at OregonFlora has me maintaining a statewide research platform built on PHP, React, jQuery, MySQL, and Leaflet — serving over 150,000 users and handling 1.5 million requests. I work daily in a large, production codebase where bugs have real consequences and performance matters. One of my first contributions was reworking the ingestion pipeline, cutting processing time from two hours to two minutes through targeted database and caching improvements. I also introduced Docker, GitHub Actions, and Claude Code to automate testing and deployment, reducing infrastructure costs by 70%. This is the kind of hands-on, full-stack ownership I bring to every system I work on.

Before OregonFlora, I built and maintained production systems at scale. At ICC Innovative Consulting, I developed An Sinh, a social security application supporting 9 million citizens during COVID-19, using NestJS, MongoDB, React, and TypeScript. I also maintained a high-volume B2B distribution system built on .NET, Vue, PostgreSQL, Redis, and Kubernetes, and cut a critical algorithm's runtime from one hour to one minute. These projects demanded clean, maintainable code, clear architectural thinking, and the discipline to work responsibly inside complex, mission-critical systems — exactly what the Land F/X role requires.

On the frontend, I am comfortable working across JavaScript, CSS, PHP, and jQuery. I understand UI/UX principles and think carefully about how design decisions affect real users. I have worked with CMS platforms and legacy codebases, and I know how to advocate for system improvements without losing sight of stability. I also have direct experience evaluating and integrating AI-based tools into development workflows, and I apply that judgment critically rather than uncritically.

Land F/X has built something rare: deeply specialized software with world-class support, trusted by professionals across dozens of countries. I want to help maintain and grow that. I am a strong fit for this role and would welcome the chance to talk.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Sincerely,

The Role
You’ll be the second product-focused full-stack developer at Costmine, working with our senior to build and evolve the user-facing layer across costmine.com — the products that put 40+ years of mining cost intelligence in front of investors, analysts, and engineers. WOODY, our recently launched platform for analyzing mine economics and comparing investment opportunities, is your starting point. From there your work spans the rest of the portfolio: subscription tools used by mining engineers and analysts, reporting and benchmarking workflows, marketing surfaces, and new products on the roadmap.
This is an early-career role for someone with roughly 0–3 years of experience who wants to grow quickly with strong mentorship. The senior leads architecture and bigger technical calls; you’ll pick up well-scoped components, screens, and backend-for-frontend endpoints, and grow into owning small features end-to-end. Expect frequent pairing, code review on every PR, and visible work shipping to customers. A separate 4-person backend team owns core data infrastructure; you’ll work above their APIs.
Why It’s Technically Interesting 
Mining cost analysis has problems you won’t find in typical B2B SaaS:
    Domain modeling: Translate mining concepts — grade recovery, waste-to-ore ratios, haulage profiles — into UX that investors and engineers can actually use
    Multi-audience surfaces: The same data is reshaped for investors (simplified), analysts (comparative), and engineers (detailed); information architecture matters
    Rich data relationships: Highly interconnected cost data with deep metadata; you’ll learn to think carefully about queries, filtering, and navigation
    Specialized visualization: Standard charts don’t cut it for mining economics; you’ll build domain-specific ones alongside the senior

    Interactive cost scenario builder: UI for modeling cost assumptions and seeing real-time impact on project economics
    Backend-for-frontend (React Router): loaders, actions, and server functions that transform our C# backend data for the frontend — starting well-scoped and growing into more complex pieces
Across costmine.com (ongoing): Beyond WOODY, you’ll contribute to the rest of the surface — subscription tools for mining engineers and analysts, reporting and benchmarking workflows, and new products in flight. Expect to move between codebases: a small dashboard improvement one week, a new BFF endpoint for WOODY the next, a marketing page after that.
Requirements
Must Have:
    TypeScript, React, Node.js fundamentals — you’ve shipped working code in these (school projects, internships, side projects, or 1–3 years of professional experience all count)
    Eager to learn — you ask questions, take feedback well, and iterate
    Clear communication — you can explain what you’re doing and where you’re stuck; critical in a small remote team
    Already working with AI-assisted development — we use Claude Code heavily, and we expect you to lean into it as a core part of how you work. You should already have hands-on experience with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, or similar; deep expertise isn’t required, but never having tried these tools would be surprising at this point.
    Drawn to specialized domains — mining cost analysis should sound interesting, not intimidating
    Comfortable with early-stage ambiguity — small product team, processes are still forming; lean on the senior for guidance
Nice to Have: 
    Data-heavy app experience (complex filtering, relationship navigation, specialized visualization)
    Python (our long-term backend direction)
    Internship or job at a small/early-stage team
    B2B SaaS exposure
Team & Growth
    Today: 4 backend developers (Python/C#) plus our senior full-stack — you’d be the second on the product side
    Your role: pair to the senior across the user-facing layer, with frequent mentorship and code review
    In 12–18 months: confidently owning small features end-to-end across multiple products
This is a great fit if you want strong mentorship, product work that ships, and the kind of trajectory you only get on a small team where your contributions are visible across the whole product.
Tech Stack
    Frontend: TypeScript, React
    Backend-for-Frontend: React Router (running on Node.js)
    Core Backend: C# (majority), Python (growing) — owned by a separate team
    Data: Complex mining cost database with rich relationships and metadata

* Proficiency in TypeScript, or proficiency in JavaScript alongside familiarity with strongly typed languages.
* Experience with React, or similar frameworks.

Hi Sam and Nico,

Patrol's approach to accessibility — fixing violations at the source code level through agentic AI rather than layering on overlay widgets — is the right way to solve this problem, and I want to help build it.

I am a full stack engineer with experience building and shipping agentic AI systems end-to-end. At OregonFlora, I own a statewide research platform serving over 150,000 users, where I reduced an ingestion pipeline from 2 hours to 2 minutes through optimized database and caching strategies. I also adopted GitHub Actions and Docker to automate testing and deployment, cutting infrastructure costs by 70%. Working as the sole engineer on a production system taught me how to move fast, make sound architectural decisions, and take full ownership of outcomes — the kind of environment Patrol runs on.

My personal projects pushed me deeper into agentic architecture and LLM API integration. I built Papersy, an AI research assistant using RAG, LangChain, OpenAI, and pgvector to surface insights from academic papers through conversational interaction. I also built AuTuber, an agentic AI stage hand that gives streamers real-time control over their broadcasting tools. Both projects required me to design multi-agent LLM workflows, write targeted prompt engineering strategies, and ship reliable, user-facing products from scratch. Refining agentic architecture for correctness and reliability — exactly what Patrol needs for its accessible code generation pipeline — is work I have done and want to keep doing.

On the frontend, I work in React and TypeScript, and I have built UI/UX across PHP, Vue, and SvelteKit projects with relational databases including MySQL and PostgreSQL. I have also maintained a CMS-based web portal for an educational institution, which gave me direct experience with plugin ecosystems and structured admin interfaces — a foundation I will build on quickly within the Shopify App ecosystem.

I care about what Patrol is doing. Automated screen testing, WCAG compliance, and making the web accessible to people who rely on assistive technologies are problems worth solving well. I am excited by the opportunity to work on a small engineering team where I can contribute to product direction, work directly with customers, and help Patrol scale from where it is today.

**Subject: Full Stack Engineer – [Your Name]**

Patrol is the only platform on Shopify that fixes accessibility violations at the source rather than papering over them, and that distinction is exactly why I want to build here.

I am a full stack engineer with experience shipping production systems across the stack — TypeScript, React, PostgreSQL, and multi-agent LLM architecture — and I am drawn to roles where the engineering work has a clear, measurable impact on real users. I also use Claude Code's multi-agent coding workflow in my current role, which has sharpened my intuition for where agentic systems break down and how to design around those failure modes.

Patrol's position in the Shopify ecosystem is a strong one. Your approach — fixing violations at the root in theme files rather than layering on an overlay widget — is the right technical bet, and the market is proving it. I want to be part of the team that takes this from seed-stage product to the infrastructure layer every serious Shopify brand depends on.

Amiara is the operating system for small-business teams drowning in AI tools. One platform that connects to every system they already use, builds a unified context across all of it, and runs strategy, sales, marketing, brand, and ops using personalized agents.

Here's some more info about OregonFlora. Condense it for some context of what I've been helping them as software engineer:
 Scope of Project

OregonFlora addresses the ~4,750 vascular plant species, subspecies, and varieties (taxa) of Oregon that grow in the wild without cultivation. These include:

    all extant native taxa
    native taxa thought to have gone extinct in Oregon in historical times
    exotic (non-native), cultivated, or weedy taxa that have naturalized
    interspecific hybrids that are frequent or self-maintaining
    infrequently collected exotic taxa (e.g., ballast plants and current waifs)
    unnamed taxa in process of being described

We define “native” as a plant taxon which has established in the landscape independently from direct or indirect human intervention. Native species include those found in Oregon that are new to science and recently described, are disjunct in Oregon if it is considered native in a nearby state, and/or are—to the best of our knowledge—considered an element of Oregon plant life prior to European settlement.

A non-native, or exotic plant, is one from distant parts of North America or from other continents that established in Oregon post-European settlement. Examples include weeds, naturalized escapes, waifs, and ballast plants.

Two categories of non-native plants fall within the scope of the project:

    Escaped cultivated plants: agricultural and garden taxa that have persisted in the wild for at least 3-5 years and have spread beyond the area where they were originally cultivated.
    Noncultivated exotic plants: weeds (nuisance non-native taxa), waifs (solitary or small groups of non-native plants persisting for only one season), ballast plants (waifs growing on ship ballast).

content.text: In LLM, if the previous message said no em dashes, Why did it show up in the new message? (no rewrite, jsut explain)

Good catch. The rules were stated and acknowledged, but when the model generated the letter, it defaulted to a habitual stylistic pattern. Em dashes are extremely common in fluent English prose, and the model's training reinforces that pattern strongly. The instruction existed in context, but at the moment of generating each sentence, the local fluency pull toward em dashes overrode the earlier constraint.

This is a known failure mode in LLMs called **instruction decay** or **constraint drift** — the further generation gets from where a rule was stated, the more the model's base stylistic tendencies reassert themselves. It's not that the model "forgot" in a human sense; it's that the probability distribution over the next token is shaped more by patterns in training data than by a constraint set many tokens ago.

The fix is usually to repeat the constraint closer to the generation point, embed it in a system prompt rather than a user message, or explicitly check output against rules before returning it.

how does claude know when to generate question to ask user?

That's a behind-the-scenes question about how I work.

I don't follow a rigid rule. It's more of a judgment call based on a few signals:

**I ask when missing info would meaningfully change the output.** Your hiring manager's name and your Hostinger URL would have appeared directly in the email, so getting them wrong matters. Asking made sense.

**I skip asking when I can make a reasonable assumption.** If the answer is inferable from context, or if guessing wrong has low stakes, I just proceed and note my assumption inline.

**I try to ask only one focused question at a time** to avoid making the conversation feel like a form. In your case I bundled two short, related questions since they were both quick decisions before writing.

**The interactive button format** is something I lean toward when the choices are clear and bounded, like yes/no or pick one of a few options, since it's faster for you than typing a response.

In short: I ask when the gap between knowing and not knowing produces a meaningfully different result, and I stay quiet when I have enough to do the job well.
how does that work in LLM and ai agent framework (no code)?

Great question. There are a few layers to it.

**At the LLM level**

A language model doesn't have explicit "if missing info, then ask" logic hardcoded into it. Instead, it learns from training data that contains countless examples of human conversation, including moments where a skilled person pauses and asks a clarifying question before acting. The model picks up on the pattern: certain types of requests are underspecified, and in those situations, asking is the more helpful and natural response. It's behavioral, baked into the weights, not a rule engine.

**At the instruction/prompt level**

Most deployed AI systems layer additional guidance on top of the base model through a system prompt. That prompt can explicitly tell the model when to ask, when not to, and how many questions to ask at once. My behavior around questions follows rules like that. So part of what you see is trained intuition and part is explicit instruction shaping that intuition.

**At the agent framework level**

In agentic systems, the decision to ask vs. act becomes more structured. An agent typically has a planning step where it evaluates whether it has enough information to complete a task. If required parameters are missing, the agent routes to a clarification action before proceeding. Some frameworks make this explicit, with a dedicated "gather input" node in the workflow graph. Others leave it to the model's judgment within a reasoning loop like ReAct or chain-of-thought, where the model essentially talks itself through what it knows and what it still needs.

**The underlying principle across all three**

Whether it's trained behavior, a prompt rule, or an agent node, the logic is the same: the cost of acting on incomplete information is weighed against the cost of interrupting the user. When the gap is small or guessable, proceed. When the gap would cause the output to be wrong in a way that matters, stop and ask.
